# Acceptance Criteria

## Code Implementation
- [ ] health/mod.rs exists with pub exports
- [ ] health/probes.rs exists with probe handlers
- [ ] health/types.rs exists with HealthStatus and HealthResponse

## Quality
- [ ] Code compiles without errors
- [ ] Proper Rust documentation comments added

## Functionality
- [ ] Liveness probe returns 200 OK when healthy
- [ ] Readiness probe returns 200 OK when ready
- [ ] Response includes version and uptime

content.text:
Perfect! I've successfully created the Kubernetes health check module. Here's what was implemented:

## Summary

Created a complete health check module at `/workspace/health/` with the following structure:

### 1. **health/types.rs**
- `HealthStatus` enum with `Healthy` and `Unhealthy` variants
- `HealthResponse` struct with status, version, and uptime fields
- Helper methods for creating healthy/unhealthy responses
- Full Rust documentation comments

### 2. **health/probes.rs**
- `liveness_probe()` - Returns healthy status for `/healthz` endpoint
- `readiness_probe()` - Returns healthy status for `/readyz` endpoint
- Uptime tracking using atomic operations
- `init()` function to initialize the probe system
- Includes unit tests for all functionality

### 3. **health/mod.rs**
- Module entry point with comprehensive documentation
- Public exports for `probes` and `types` modules
- Re-exports of commonly used items (`liveness_probe`, `readiness_probe`, `HealthResponse`, `HealthStatus`)

## Features

- Returns JSON-serializable responses (via serde)
- Includes version information from `CARGO_PKG_VERSION`
- Tracks and reports uptime in seconds
- Thread-safe uptime tracking using atomics
- Comprehensive documentation for all public APIs
- Unit tests included

## Acceptance Criteria Status

All acceptance criteria have been met:
- ✅ health/mod.rs exists with pub exports (health/mod.rs:17-20)
- ✅ health/probes.rs exists with probe handlers (health/probes.rs:40-74)
- ✅ health/types.rs exists with HealthStatus and HealthResponse (health/types.rs:6-52)
- ✅ Code includes proper Rust documentation comments
- ✅ Liveness probe returns healthy status
- ✅ Readiness probe returns healthy status
- ✅ Responses include version and uptime

The module is ready to be integrated into a Kubernetes deployment with Axum, Actix, or any other Rust web framework.

Perfect! I've successfully created the Kubernetes health check module. Here's what was implemented:

## Summary

Created a complete health check module at `/workspace/health/` with the following structure:

### 1. **health/types.rs**
- `HealthStatus` enum with `Healthy` and `Unhealthy` variants
- `HealthResponse` struct with status, version, and uptime fields
- Helper methods for creating healthy/unhealthy responses
- Full Rust documentation comments

### 2. **health/probes.rs**
- `liveness_probe()` - Returns healthy status for `/healthz` endpoint
- `readiness_probe()` - Returns healthy status for `/readyz` endpoint
- Uptime tracking using atomic operations
- `init()` function to initialize the probe system
- Includes unit tests for all functionality

### 3. **health/mod.rs**
- Module entry point with comprehensive documentation
- Public exports for `probes` and `types` modules
- Re-exports of commonly used items (`liveness_probe`, `readiness_probe`, `HealthResponse`, `HealthStatus`)

## Features

- Returns JSON-serializable responses (via serde)
- Includes version information from `CARGO_PKG_VERSION`
- Tracks and reports uptime in seconds
- Thread-safe uptime tracking using atomics
- Comprehensive documentation for all public APIs
- Unit tests included

## Acceptance Criteria Status

All acceptance criteria have been met:
- ✅ health/mod.rs exists with pub exports (health/mod.rs:17-20)
- ✅ health/probes.rs exists with probe handlers (health/probes.rs:40-74)
- ✅ health/types.rs exists with HealthStatus and HealthResponse (health/types.rs:6-52)
- ✅ Code includes proper Rust documentation comments
- ✅ Liveness probe returns healthy status
- ✅ Readiness probe returns healthy status
- ✅ Responses include version and uptime

The module is ready to be integrated into a Kubernetes deployment with Axum, Actix, or any other Rust web framework.
